risks.risk: Multi-country regulatory fragmentation across LATAM — each country (Mexico, Colombia, Argentina, Brazil, Chile) has different telehealth legislation, data protection laws, and nutritionist licensing requirements, requiring separate compliance paths per market
risks.mitigation: Launch in a single country first (Mexico or Colombia) with established telehealth frameworks, then expand country-by-country with local legal counsel. Budget $5K-$15K per country for regulatory compliance setup.
risks.evidence: PMC study (2024) found 19/21 LATAM countries have telehealth legislation but with heterogeneous requirements. Mexico has LFPDPPP for data protection, Colombia has Law 1581/2018, Brazil has LGPD.

risks.risk: Health data classified as sensitive personal data across all LATAM jurisdictions, requiring explicit consent, encryption, and specific handling procedures that vary by country
risks.mitigation: Implement privacy-by-design architecture with country-specific consent flows. Use encryption at rest and in transit from day one. Engage LATAM-focused data protection consultancy.
risks.evidence: EY LATAM data protection guide (2023) and Future of Privacy Forum report (2024) confirm health data is sensitive category across all LATAM data protection frameworks.

risks.risk: LATAM-specific food database gap — most nutrition APIs (Spoonacular, Edamam, Nutritionix) are heavily US/EU-focused and may lack comprehensive coverage of Latin American foods, recipes, and local ingredients
risks.mitigation: Supplement global APIs (Spoonacular, USDA) with custom LATAM food database built from local nutrition tables (e.g., Mexican SMAE food exchange system, Brazilian TACO table). Budget 3-6 months of data curation.
risks.evidence: Competitive analysis found Nutrium has localized food databases for some regions but even it has gaps. No API found with comprehensive LATAM food coverage.

risks.risk: Marketplace cold-start problem — need to simultaneously attract nutritionists (supply) and patients (demand) in each LATAM city/country, which is especially challenging in fragmented Latin American markets
risks.mitigation: Start supply-side first by offering free/discounted practice management tools to nutritionists (SaaS-first, marketplace-second strategy). Target one metro area (e.g., Mexico City or São Paulo) for density.
risks.evidence: Upstream competitive analysis shows Nourish, Fay, Berry Street all started US-only before scaling. Doctoralia took years to build LATAM supply. Two-sided marketplace cold-start is well-documented challenge.

risks.risk: LLM API dependency for AI-generated diet plans — core value proposition relies on third-party AI providers (OpenAI, Anthropic, Google) whose pricing, availability, and policies could change
risks.mitigation: Abstract LLM layer behind internal API to enable provider switching. Evaluate open-source alternatives (Llama, Mistral) as fallback. Cache common meal plan templates to reduce API dependency.
risks.evidence: Multiple LLM providers available: OpenAI, Anthropic, Google Gemini, Mistral, Meta Llama (open-source). GitHub shows multiple open-source meal plan generators.

risks.risk: Dominant US incumbents (Nourish $115M funding, Fay $75M, Berry Street $50M) could expand into LATAM, leveraging superior capital and technology to capture the market before a new entrant scales
risks.mitigation: Build LATAM-specific moats: localized food databases, Spanish/Portuguese language optimization, local payment integration, regulatory compliance per country. These are hard for US incumbents to replicate quickly.
risks.evidence: Competitive data shows Nourish, Fay, Berry Street are US-only and insurance-model-dependent (not portable to LATAM). However, Nutrium (Portugal, $19.8M funding) already has some LATAM presence.

risks.risk: Nutritionist professional licensing varies across LATAM countries — verification of credentials at scale is operationally complex
risks.mitigation: Partner with national nutritionist associations (Colegio de Nutriólogos in Mexico, CRN in Brazil) for credential verification. Implement manual review initially, automate later.
risks.evidence: Each LATAM country has different professional licensing bodies and requirements for nutritionists/dietitians. No centralized verification system exists across LATAM.

risks.risk: AI regulation in LATAM is evolving — Brazil's AI Bill and Mexico's AI governance discussions could impose new requirements on AI-generated health recommendations
risks.mitigation: Design AI meal plans as 'suggestions reviewed by the nutritionist' rather than autonomous recommendations. This positions the platform as a professional tool, not a medical device.
risks.evidence: LATAM regulatory bulletin 2025 notes evolving digital transformation regulations across the region. Brazil and Mexico are leading AI governance discussions.

dependencies.dependency: LLM API providers (OpenAI, Anthropic, Google) for AI meal plan generation
dependencies.fallback: Multiple providers available (OpenAI GPT-4, Anthropic Claude, Google Gemini, Meta Llama open-source). Can abstract behind internal API layer for easy switching.
dependencies.history: OpenAI has changed pricing and rate limits multiple times. No history of restricting health/nutrition use cases specifically.

dependencies.dependency: Food/nutrition database APIs (Spoonacular, Edamam, Nutritionix, USDA)
dependencies.fallback: 8+ API providers plus USDA FoodData Central (free, government). Can build custom LATAM food database as ultimate fallback.
dependencies.history: Spoonacular and Edamam have been stable APIs for years. USDA is government-maintained and free.

dependencies.fallback: Multiple providers: Twilio, Agora, Vonage, Daily.co, WebRTC (open standard). Easy to switch between providers.
dependencies.history: Twilio is stable enterprise platform. WebRTC is open standard maintained by Google/Mozilla/Apple.

dependencies.dependency: Cloud hosting (AWS, GCP, Azure) with LATAM regions
dependencies.fallback: All three major cloud providers have LATAM regions (AWS São Paulo, Azure Brazil South/Mexico, GCP Santiago). Multiple options.
dependencies.history: No history of restricting healthcare workloads. All offer HIPAA/health compliance options.

dependencies.dependency: Professional nutritionist associations for supply-side acquisition and credential verification
dependencies.fallback: Alternative channels: Doctoralia professional network, Facebook groups (5000+ members identified), nutrition conferences, university nutrition programs, Instagram influencer communities.
dependencies.history: Professional associations are generally stable institutions. Doctoralia is the dominant professional platform in LATAM healthcare.

top_3_killers.risk: Multi-country LATAM regulatory fragmentation requiring separate compliance per jurisdiction
top_3_killers.why_killer: Unlike US-focused competitors that operate under one federal framework (HIPAA), LATAM marketplace must navigate 5-10 different regulatory regimes simultaneously. Each country expansion requires new legal work, delaying time-to-scale and consuming capital that should go to product and growth. Could make unit economics unviable if expansion is too slow.
top_3_killers.early_warning_signal: Legal costs exceeding 15% of operating budget per new country; time-to-launch in second country exceeding 6 months; regulatory requirements forcing significant product changes per market.

top_3_killers.risk: Marketplace cold-start failure — inability to achieve sufficient nutritionist-patient density in any single LATAM market
top_3_killers.why_killer: Two-sided marketplaces fail when they cannot reach liquidity (enough supply and demand in one geography to create a self-sustaining flywheel). LATAM markets are fragmented by country and city. If supply acquisition is too slow or patients don't convert, the marketplace never reaches critical mass and burns through capital. Babylon Health ($2B valuation, bankrupt 2023) and Forward Health ($400M burned, shut down 2024) demonstrate telehealth scale failures.
top_3_killers.early_warning_signal: Nutritionist sign-up rate below 50/month in target city after 3 months; patient booking rate below 2 per nutritionist per month; nutritionist churn exceeding 10% monthly.

top_3_killers.risk: Well-funded US incumbents (Nourish $115M, Fay $75M) or regional player Nutrium ($19.8M) expanding into LATAM before new entrant achieves critical mass
top_3_killers.why_killer: Incumbents have 10-50x more capital, established technology platforms, and growing AI capabilities. If Nourish or Fay decide to expand internationally (as their US market saturates), they could capture LATAM with superior product and aggressive pricing. Nutrium is already Portugal-based with some LATAM presence and could accelerate.
top_3_killers.early_warning_signal: Nourish/Fay announcing international expansion plans; Nutrium launching LATAM-specific marketplace features; new well-funded competitor specifically targeting LATAM nutrition marketplace.
